summary:
Shock in Trauma

Shock: Definition

Shock is a state of inadequate tissue perfusion and oxygen delivery to meet cellular metabolic demands. It is not simply low blood pressure — it is circulatory failure at the cellular level. By the time hypotension develops, the patient has exhausted compensatory mechanisms and is in decompensated shock.

Four Types of Shock

TypePrimary DefectTrauma Causes
HypovolemicLoss of circulating blood volumeHemorrhage (most common in trauma), burns (fluid shift)
DistributiveLoss of vascular tone (vasodilation)Spinal cord injury (neurogenic shock), sepsis, anaphylaxis
CardiogenicPump failureCardiac contusion, MI, tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade
ObstructivePhysical obstruction of blood flowTension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade
Key Concept: In trauma, hypovolemic shock from hemorrhage is most common. But always consider tension pneumothorax (obstructive) and spinal cord injury (neurogenic) — their treatment is completely different.

Hemorrhagic Shock Classification (ATLS)

Average adult blood volume ≈ 5 L (70 mL/kg). Classification by % blood loss:

ParameterClass IClass IIClass IIIClass IV
% Loss≤15% (<750 mL)15-30% (750-1,500 mL)30-40% (1,500-2,000 mL)>40% (>2,000 mL)
HR<100>100-120120-140, thready>140 or bradycardia (ominous)
BPNormalNormal (narrowed pulse pressure)Hypotension (SBP <90)Profound hypotension (SBP <70-80)
RR14-2020-2828-35>35 or agonal
Mental StatusAnxious, alertAnxious, agitatedConfused, anxiousLethargic, obtunded, unconscious
SkinPink, warm, dryCool, pale, diaphoreticCool, pale, clammy, mottledCold, ashen, cyanotic, waxy
Cap Refill<2 sec2-3 sec>3 secAbsent
Urine Output>30 mL/hr20-30 mL/hr5-15 mL/hr<5 mL/hr
NREMT Exam Pearl: Know Class III (30-40% loss, 1,500-2,000 mL) — the threshold where compensation fails and hypotension develops. Class I and II = compensated shock. Class III = decompensated shock.

Compensated vs Decompensated vs Irreversible Shock

Compensated (Class I-II): Body uses neuroendocrine mechanisms — baroreceptor reflex (↑ HR, ↑ contractility, ↑ vasoconstriction), hormonal responses (ADH, RAAS, catecholamines). Key feature: SBP is maintained. Patient may have normal BP but is tachycardic, pale, cool, diaphoretic.

Critical NREMT Point: A trauma patient can have normal vital signs while in compensated shock. Treat the mechanism, not just the vitals. A femur + pelvic fracture patient with normal BP but tachycardia and pallor is in compensated shock. Do not be falsely reassured.

Decompensated (Class III): >30% loss. Compensatory mechanisms overwhelmed. SBP <90, worsening tachycardia, confusion/agitation, marked vasoconstriction, narrow pulse pressure (<30 mmHg), oliguria.
Irreversible (Class IV): >40% loss. Profound hypotension, preterminal bradycardia, unconscious, agonal respirations, PEA. Cellular damage is so severe that resuscitation is unlikely to succeed.

Fluid Resuscitation

Permissive Hypotension (Balanced Resuscitation)

Goal: maintain adequate perfusion (SBP ~80-90 or palpable radial pulse) without raising BP enough to disrupt clots. Aggressive fluids → dilute clotting factors, worsen coagulopathy, and can "pop the clot."
Contraindicated in TBI: TBI patients need SBP ≥100-110 mmHg to maintain cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) and prevent secondary brain injury.
Fluid: 500 mL NS bolus over 10-15 minutes. Reassess. Repeat once if needed. Limit to 2 L total crystalloid.

NREMT Fluid Bolus Guideline: 500 mL NS if SBP <90 OR signs of shock (tachycardia + pallor/diaphoresis/AMS). Reassess. Repeat once if needed. Limit 2 L crystalloid. Warm fluids preferred.

Shock Differential in Trauma

FeatureHypovolemicNeurogenicTension PTXTamponade
SkinPale, cool, clammyWarm, dry, flushedPale, coolPale, cool
HRTachyBradycardiaTachyTachy
Pulse PressureNarrowWideNarrowNarrow (pulsus paradoxus)
Breath SoundsNormalNormalDecreased unilateralNormal
TracheaMidlineMidlineDeviated awayMidline
JVDFlatFlatDistendedDistended
KeyBleeding sourceSCI + brady + warm skinTracheal deviation + unilateral BS + JVDMuffled hearts + JVD + hypotension (Beck's triad)

EMS Provider Management of Shock

  1. Hemorrhage control FIRST — tourniquets, pressure dressings, wound packing before anything else
  2. High-flow O₂ — titrate to SpO₂ ≥94%
  3. Large-bore IV × 2 (14-16G) or IO if unable
  4. 500 mL NS bolus if SBP <90 or shock signs — reassess, repeat once, limit 2 L
  5. Permissive hypotension — unless TBI (target SBP ≥100-110)
  6. Keep warm — hypothermia worsens coagulopathy
  7. Scoop and run — definitive treatment is in the OR
  8. Notify trauma center with mechanism, vitals, interventions

quiz.q: A 24-year-old male with a stab wound to the RUQ has BP 106/70, HR 118, RR 24, pale diaphoretic skin. He is anxious. What class of hemorrhagic shock is this?
quiz.explanation: Class II: HR 118 (tachy), normal BP, narrowed pulse pressure, pale/diaphoretic skin. Normal BP with these findings indicates compensated shock.

quiz.q: A 45-year-old in an MVC has GCS 10, SBP 120/70, HR 104, deformed left thigh. What should guide your fluid management?
options: Aggressive fluids for SBP ≥100 due to head injury
One 500 mL bolus regardless of vitals
quiz.explanation: GCS ≤13 indicates possible TBI. TBI requires SBP ≥100-110 to maintain CPP. Permissive hypotension is contraindicated in TBI.

quiz.q: [HARD] A 32-year-old female fell and has BP 86/54, HR 112, warm dry skin on legs but cool pale skin on arms. She has decreased sensation and motor function below the nipple line. What shock type is most likely?
quiz.explanation: Warm dry skin below injury level + hypotension + spinal cord deficit at T4 = neurogenic shock. Warm/dry skin is the key differentiator from hemorrhagic shock (cool/pale/clammy).

flashcards.question: What are the four types of shock and which is most common in trauma?
flashcards.answer: (1) Hypovolemic — loss of volume (MOST COMMON). (2) Distributive — loss of vascular tone (neurogenic, sepsis). (3) Cardiogenic — pump failure. (4) Obstructive — physical obstruction (tension PTX, tamponade).

flashcards.question: Differentiate compensated vs decompensated shock.
flashcards.answer: Compensated (Class I-II, <30% loss): SBP maintained through tachycardia, vasoconstriction, increased contractility. Decompensated (Class III, >30%): Hypotension develops, worsening tachycardia, altered mental status.

flashcards.question: Describe the four ATLS hemorrhagic shock classes.
flashcards.answer: Class I: ≤15% (<750 mL), HR <100, normal BP. Class II: 15-30% (750-1,500 mL), HR >100-120, normal BP, narrowed pulse pressure. Class III: 30-40% (1,500-2,000 mL), HR 120-140, HYPOTENSION (SBP <90). Class IV: >40% (>2,000 mL), HR >140 or brady, profound hypotension.

flashcards.question: What is permissive hypotension and when is it contraindicated?
flashcards.answer: Target SBP ~80-90 mmHg to maintain perfusion without disrupting clots. CONTRAINDICATED in TBI — TBI patients need SBP ≥100-110 mmHg to maintain cerebral perfusion pressure.

flashcards.question: Differentiate neurogenic from hemorrhagic shock.
flashcards.answer: Neurogenic: Bradycardia, warm/dry/flushed skin, wide pulse pressure, spinal cord injury. Hemorrhagic: Tachycardia, pale/cool/clammy skin, narrow pulse pressure, evidence of bleeding.

flashcards.question: What is the NREMT fluid bolus guideline?
flashcards.answer: 500 mL NS if SBP <90 OR signs of shock (tachycardia + pallor/diaphoresis/AMS). Reassess. Repeat once if needed. Limit 2 L crystalloid. Permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90) unless TBI.

flashcards.question: Differentiate tension pneumothorax from cardiac tamponade.
flashcards.answer: Tension PTX: Unilateral decreased BS, tracheal deviation AWAY, JVD, hypotension. Tamponade: Muffled heart tones + JVD + hypotension (Beck's triad), pulsus paradoxus, trachea midline, normal BS.

flashcards.question: What are the key prehospital interventions for shock?
flashcards.answer: (1) Hemorrhage control first. (2) High-flow O₂. (3) Large-bore IV × 2. (4) 500 mL NS if SBP <90. (5) Prevent hypothermia. (6) Scoop and run. (7) Notify trauma center.

critical.scenario: A 35-year-old male was shot once in the right upper chest. Initial: BP 102/74, HR 116, RR 26, SpO₂ 95%, breath sounds clear bilaterally, no tracheal deviation, neck veins flat. Over 10 minutes, BP drops to 88/60, HR 130, confused, SpO₂ 89%. Neck veins now distended, trachea midline but right chest hyperresonant to percussion.
critical.question: What is the most likely cause of this rapid decompensation?

options.t: Hemorrhagic shock from internal chest bleed — increase IV fluids, give second bolus, expedite transport
options.f: Incorrect. Distended neck veins + hyperresonance + hypotension from penetrating chest wound = tension pneumothorax, not just hemorrhagic shock. Fluids alone will not resolve it.

options.t: Tension pneumothorax from one-way valve mechanism — immediate needle decompression (14G, 2nd ICS MCL or 4th-5th ICS MAL) needed before or during transport
options.f: Correct. Penetrating chest wound + progressive hypotension + distended neck veins + tympany = tension pneumothorax. Needle decompression is life-saving.

critical.explanation: Tension pneumothorax: trapped air compresses vena cava (↓ preload → hypotension), heart (↓ CO), and contralateral lung (↓ ventilation → hypoxia). Needle decompression (14G angiocath) at 2nd ICS MCL or 4th-5th ICS MAL. Rush of air confirms diagnosis.
critical.kpi: Tension pneumothorax recognition and needle decompression

critical.scenario: A 58-year-old female, restrained driver in a 35 mph frontal MVC. Seat belt contusion across chest/abdomen. Initial: BP 100/68, HR 108, pale/diaphoretic, mild epigastric tenderness, GCS 15. 15 min into transport: BP 84/52, HR 126, confused, vomiting, visible abdominal distention.
critical.question: What is the source of her decompensation and how should you adjust management?

options.t: Seat belt contusion is soft-tissue only — vomiting is from pain; give second bolus and continue
options.f: Incorrect. Seat belt sign across abdomen is a known marker for intra-abdominal injury. Progressive distention + hypotension + vomiting = abdominal hemorrhage.

options.t: Suspect intra-abdominal hemorrhage from liver/spleen/mesenteric tear — give second 500 mL NS bolus (permissive hypotension, SBP 80-90), prevent hypothermia, update trauma center
options.f: Correct. Seat belt sign + progressive distention + falling BP = intra-abdominal hemorrhage. Permissive hypotension unless TBI. Warm the patient, limit crystalloid to 2L.

critical.explanation: Seat belt sign is a well-known marker for intra-abdominal injury — energy through the belt can lacerate liver/spleen or tear mesenteric vessels. Deterioration from compensated to decompensated shock = ongoing hemorrhage. Permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90) prevents clot disruption.

critical.scenario: A 26-year-old restrained passenger in a rollover MVC (3 rolls, no ejection). Complains of neck and back pain. BP 88/56, HR 100, RR 20, SpO₂ 99%. Skin is warm and dry. Thoracic spine tenderness (T4-T6). Cannot feel anything below nipples, no leg movement, minimal arm movement. Hands are warm and well-perfused.
critical.question: How do you differentiate neurogenic from hemorrhagic shock, and what is the appropriate management?

options.t: Hemorrhagic shock from internal bleeding — aggressive fluids, PASG/MAST if available
options.f: Incorrect. Warm dry skin + relative bradycardia (HR 100 with SBP 88) + spinal cord deficit = neurogenic shock. Aggressive fluids alone may not correct neurogenic shock.

options.t: Neurogenic shock from thoracic SCI — loss of sympathetic tone below T4 causes vasodilation. 500 mL NS bolus, spinal precautions, recognize possible concurrent hemorrhagic shock (MVC mechanism). ALS vasopressors may be needed
options.f: Correct. Neurogenic shock: hypotension + warm/dry skin + bradycardia + SCI. But concurrent hemorrhagic shock cannot be ruled out. Cautious fluids, spinal immobilization, ALS for vasopressors.

critical.explanation: Neurogenic shock: disruption of sympathetic outflow → unopposed vagal tone (bradycardia) + loss of vasoconstriction (warm/dry skin, hypotension). Key differentiators from hemorrhagic: warm/dry skin (not cool/pale), bradycardia (not tachy), wide pulse pressure (not narrow), known SCI. Manage with fluids + vasopressors. Concurrent hemorrhagic shock possible.
